# Thread: PMS and/or PMDD
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pms-and-or-pmdd.20522/>
**Subforum:** Female Quantum Biology
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ashley — 2017-11-29T03:18:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pms-and-or-pmdd.20522/post-229086>

Hi Everyone!

I'm new(er) here. Biggest thing Dr. Jack mentioned to focus on for now with my specific symptoms is getting as much sun as possible - and I've been getting at least 1 hour a day, most days closer to 3/4 as my schedule allows (a bit more [here on my start](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/do-i-meet-the-requirements-for-the-leptin-reset-rx.20428/#post-228575) recently).

My current state of PMS made me remember how bad it can be. The week or two leading up to my period is the worst ever. Every month it's like I only get half of to be a functioning human. Those 1-2 weeks prior to my period I want to eat the universe, and I become a vulnerable and lazy "woe is me" personality. Although I generally struggle with overall apathy, I'm not necessarily "woe is me" in my essence. Ovulation is pretty rad though - I'm at my best on the 13-15th or so days of my cycle, I feel confident, in a flowy stable state, and overall just great. I understand testosterone is high during this time, and those first two weeks I start leaning out, feeling better/relieved. I have a vague understanding of progesterone, estrogen, test, etc during our cycles so I can see some of the correlations. I also had amenorrhea from about 2011-2012/13 when I was anorexic just as a background.

Is there any way around feeling sub-human those 1-2 weeks leading to our periods? Is this really something (most) women have to just accept and live with? It's very frustrating to me to feel like during my fertile years I get to experience half feeling good and the other half feeling dreadful. And then comes menopause....

Hi Ashley welcome.
that's definitely not something a woman should accept living with.

Blocking blue light with sunset is allso important

### Ashley — 2017-12-02T20:00:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pms-and-or-pmdd.20522/post-229367>

Thanks shiran!  I've been getting the AM sunlight and have an appointment with my optometrist and will be getting prescription blue blockers, and also am rearranging the light situation in my home. I guess I'm just curious if there are any additional changes I can make for PMS specifically if anyone has any suggestions. I understand all of this will take quite some time to see very noticeable changes, but I'm very open to making extra changes to avoid this being a continual problem.

### ScottishEmma — 2017-12-11T10:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pms-and-or-pmdd.20522/post-229983>

Hey Ashley

I totally get your symptoms. I've been the exact same way since my second child (she's nearly three). It got progressively worse up until about two months ago when we moved to Spain.

I tried lots of herbs - I still take a 'Superbitch' Tea and I also swear by DIM - it made my periods lighter and got rid of the rage.

I also upped my carbs and that totally helps. I don't care what anyone says, carbs fucking help.

I still get a cycle where after ovulation I feel less LET'S FUCKING GET ALLLLLL THE STUFF DONE but it's definitely better.

Hang in there, play around with different things but for me,

SUN/DAYLIGHT as much as possible on as much skin as possible...
DIM
CARBS
EATING AS MANY VEGETABLES AS I CAN
MEDITATION
YOGA

All helped!

### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-11T15:45:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pms-and-or-pmdd.20522/post-229993>

What implications does this have for a young couple getting married?

### Foxglove — 2018-01-19T00:48:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pms-and-or-pmdd.20522/post-232880>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=229993)
>
> Endometrium is another surface so anyone who listened to the Dec 2017 Q & A can solve for X. What happens to ladies whose endometriums are deuterium bombs: bleeding and painful periods........they are looking for a place to hide and transfer the deuterium because their stem cells are filled to the max.

This explains why I had bad periods before going keto-paleo. I learned what it was like to not have cramps, and I thought, were women always having these easy periods with no cramps? Dumbfounded.

@[Ashley](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10746/) how long have you had this? Have you been on birth control? I know people don't like to talk about it, but I've never been able to use it. I've tried all the kinds. It makes me a sad, horrible person.

I only started getting PMDD after I was 30 but recently I realized it was a couple months after getting a Mirena IUD. I found some research that the active ingredient in high amounts can tank your hormones. You can be ovulating and not have the hormones to support a pregnancy. That's scary. Your description of only getting half a month as a real person hit home! I suffered through it and used various Rx's for the symptoms, including Prozac, which I immediately dumped because it turned me into a zombie. That's when I started researching.

PMDD is more common in women with any kind of depression, SAD, or a history of trauma/PTSD. Both Prozac and dutasteride have been shown to reduce symptoms of PMS and PMDD by working on an enzyme involved with Progesterone metabolism. And they've also found a gene that makes you susceptible. It's a hormone/receptor issue and you're NOT crazy.

Where I diverged from conventional medical wisdom is I started taking Vitex, an herb known to help PMS symptoms. It made a huge difference. Doctors will tell you that you need to stop ovulation to stop the problem, so go on birth control hormones, or take headache pills and anxiety medicine. But Vitex works by promoting ovulation and raising progesterone. So, the exact opposite of the typical treatment, and it works. Because it worked, it gave me a clue and I changed my approach.

Vitex is a dopamine agonist and affects your pituitary hormones. Dopamine and hormones are what we are trying to fix with the Leptin Rx and CT! I decided I to go off it and everything else. No fake hormones, no Vitex, no drugs. I'm re-committing to morning sun (as much as you can get) and cold air. I'm in the beginning of the CT protocol. And the fact that women with PMDD are more likely to have winter depression speaks volumes to me. I also went off caffeine because it's one of the negative factors for PMS. I used Adaptogenic herbs in the past and they were great for my mood swiags.

That's where I am now, so I can't tell you yet how much of a difference it's made yet, but I already feel like a new person. I have a calmer energy and clearer head. I'm going to give my cycle a few months to regulate itself.

Been reading Kelly Brogan's "A Mind of Your Own," and it's cementing my decisions. She is a psychiatrist who doesn't use drugs anymore, and she wrote a book geared towards women with depression, blowing open all the myths about drugs and mental illness. She says that depression is an opportunity to look into your life, figure out what's wrong, and change it. She supports a Paleo lifestyle and she wants all her patients off of hormonal birth control. I'm doing the JK protocols because they're more fundamental, but her book made me feel like I wasn't alone in the world, and that counts for a lot. If you need any encouragement during your low days, maybe pick up a copy.

So, whatever happens, I feel you!

From Icelandic Glacial's FAQ page:

**Is the water derived from actual glaciers?**
No. The name “Icelandic Glacial” is inspired by the majestic glaciers of Iceland and is meant to capture the essence of our beautiful country. Icelandic Glacial is 100% natural spring water. The water is naturally filtered over thousands of years through lava rock and bottled at the underground source - the Ölfus Spring.

> of it. A big big thanks for this and I want to make a plea to Dr. K to do a written notes version with links, etc. like he said he might consider. So much valuable stuff laid out.....

If enough of you want me to do it I will...........Phillip I told you I had your goods coming. Do you hear me now?

I want to re iterated what I told you in my own journal...............I build arks for black swans before it begins to rain.

Phillips you're world is already taking on water and flooding.

I need you to understand this: What they tell is not all the truth you need to know to navigate the path you are now in.

You must change your nnEMF environment pouring deuterium into you changing the cell volumes in you chronically otherwise the cancer won't go anywhere but to other organs.

Where does my passion come from????

One of the strengths of existing paradigms/frameworks for believing/thinking and living in science is that many of them have survived the test of time. But just because something persists for a long time does not imply it is correct. Newton was right by belief for 500 years and then he wasn't because of Mercury's perihelion problem. Einstein gave us a plausible answer. We are now stuck on his beliefs for the last 120 years. I have a sense the latest data in cosmology is Einstein's perihelion problem. The red shift issue laid out by Halton Arp being a real problem for relativity. Generally speaking however, if an idea has been around throughout history, it’s often fair to assume that, whatever it is, it has a good overall reason for existing in society. This isn’t always the case, but it’s a rule of thumb that mostly holds up if you examine the history of man. As such, if you want to refute a part of an existing dogma, then you need strong intellectual confidence to do so. Mitochondriacs have this gene that remains un-deuterated!!!!

You need to trust that your reason for either rejecting or accepting a part of something else is good and honest and that it’s not going to lead you astray. You call out BS when you see it regardless of the social fall out. You limit your exposure to those who promote false hoods and support a failing paradigmeven when they don't know it. You protect people from the Dunning Kruger effect while examining and checking your own beliefs against the very same effect to see if new data destroys your new perspectives. You protect your folowers from your own biases by point them out constantly. You tell everyone no one is immune to this fallibility because science is never a fact, it is just an approxiamtion of what we currently accept is true waiting for new data to blow up the paradigm. If we don't act this way, it could be costly decision to our own biology and our tribe. ### Penny — 2017-12-04T20:53:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229490>

This is a great article:
[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/)

**Submolecular regulation of cell transformation by deuterium depleting water exchange reactions in the tricarboxylic acid substrate cycle**
[László G Boros](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Boros%20LG%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),a,b,c,\* [Dominic P. D’Agostino](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=D%26%23x02019%3BAgostino%20DP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),d [Howard E. Katz](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Katz%20HE%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),e [Justine P. Roth](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Roth%20JP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),f [Emmanuelle J. Meuillet](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Meuillet%20EJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),g,h and [Gábor Somlyai](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Somlyai%20G%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644)j
[Author information ► Copyright and License information ►](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#)
The publisher's final edited version of this article is available at [Med Hypotheses](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/elink.fcgi?dbfrom=pubmed&retmode=ref&cmd=prlinks&id=26826644)
See other articles in PMC that [cite](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/citedby/) the published article.
[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#)
**Abstract**
The naturally occurring isotope of hydrogen (1H), deuterium (2H), could have an important biological role. Deuterium depleted water delays tumor progression in mice, dogs, cats and humans. Hydratase enzymes of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle control cell growth and deplete deuterium from redox cofactors, fatty acids and DNA, which undergo hydride ion and hydrogen atom transfer reactions. A model is proposed that emphasizes the terminal complex of mitochondrial electron transport chain reducing molecular oxygen to deuterium depleted water (DDW); this affects gluconeogenesis as well as fatty acid oxidation. In the former, the DDW is thought to diminish the deuteration of sugar-phosphates in the DNA backbone, helping to preserve stability of hydrogen bond networks, possibly protecting against aneuploidy and resisting strand breaks, occurring upon exposure to radiation and certain anticancer chemotherapeutics. DDW is proposed here to link cancer prevention and treatment using natural ketogenic diets, low deuterium drinking water, as well as DDW production as the mitochondrial downstream mechanism of targeted anti-cancer drugs such as Avastin and Glivec. The role of 2H in biology is a potential missing link to the elusive cancer puzzle seemingly correlated with cancer epidemiology in western populations as a result of excessive 2H loading from processed carbohydrate intake in place of natural fat consumption.

### Penny — 2017-12-04T21:00:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229491>

from the same article - I'm confused... the metabolites that I've made bold seem to be a huge tell that the reaction involving H2 didn't work because of too much D2 - but does that also mean (last line) that glucose, lactic, glutamine and glycine if ingested will feed cancer if not enough H2 is available? Like that could be a total fail for someone taking glycine or glutamine and I think serine was also mentioned... also, it seems that an organic acids test could - maybe - identify the existence of cancer?

**Deuterium carrying oncometabolites - Consistency and specificity of associations regarding mitochondrial matrix water recycling and cancer**
The shift from genetic thinking towards cancer being a metabolic disease has been due to the application of metabolomics platforms identifying “oncometabolites”. Oncometabolites are intracellular products which accumulate, initiate and maintain uncontrolled cell growth with metastasis. One of the first oncometabolites identified is 2-hydroxyglutarate, a relatively rare metabolite that is found in high concentrations in gliomas \[[34](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R34),[35](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R35)\]. Multiple mechanisms have been suggested for oncometabolites as means of inducing cancer. Altered gene functions and signaling pathways by indirect histonemethylation contribute to oncogenesis. Since the identification of **2-hydroxyglutarate** formed in the cytoplasm, formed from citrate and isocitrate, other mitochondrial products such as **fumarate** and **succinate** in renal cell carcinomas \[[36](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R36)\] and paragangliomas, respectively have been identified. These products accumulate due to the lack of metabolic hydration to form their TCA cycle product, ketoglutarate and malate, respectively, whereby molecular crowding and the lack of metabolic water deriving hydrogen transfer to NADPH by isocitrate dehydrogenase initiate and maintain aggressive tumor growth. Therefore, “oncometabolite” formation is the results of enzymatic defects that recycle low deuterium metabolic water back into carbon cycling, gluconeogenesis and nucleic acid sugar backbone synthesis. The excessive appearance, i.e. accumulation of “metabolically dry” oncometabolites is consistent with our hypothesis that cancer is formed on the basis of mitochondrial defects that lack hydration of TCA cycle intermediates with low deuterium matrix water as the result of such defects. Such claim is supported by the fact that restoring hydratase function of mitochondria reverses tumor cells back to their genetically stable non-proliferating normal phenotype \[[37](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R37)\] with normal matrix water content, composition and morphology. All of the non-TCA cycle deriving oncometabolites arise from aerobic glycolysis, glutaminolysis or one carbon metabolic cycles \[[38](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R38)\] while carrying deuterium from the environment partially through nutrition. These include **glycine** in breast cancer, **asparagine** in leukemia, **choline** in prostate, brain, breast cancers, **glutamine** in myc-dependent cancers, as well as **glucose, serine, lactate and polyamines in most cancers. Common nutrients such as glucose, lactic, amino, glutaminic and glyceric acids become oncometabolites only when mitochondrial ketogenic deuterium depleting and/or metabolite hydrating processes are insufficient to exchange and replace deuterium on specific carbons before these carbohydrate carbon skeletons become functional and structural sugar backbones for DNA, RNA and membrane building fatty acids.**

### Penny — 2017-12-04T21:04:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229492>

from the same article:

So, I think plants put the deuterium in the sugary part of the plant like the fruit, so maybe a good move would be to eat the most bitter greens you can find: the 3 I know of in order of bitterness: chewing on olive leaves (unholy), raduchio, dandelion greens -
the common bean reference is interesting - maybe a summer hack?

**Biological coherence of the deuterium loading hypothesis of cancer**
The core reducing equivalent in living cells to produce new DNA and fatty acids via reductive carboxylation and biomolecule synthesis is NADPH and its deuterium loading properties depend on carbon-specific positional glucose phosphate deuterium enrichments, as well as deuterium enrichment of the cytoplasmic and mitochondrial water pools \[[39](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R39),[40](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R40)\]. The above mechanisms are all important because intramolecular deuterium distributions reveal disequilibrium and a chemical shift axis for example in fructose-6-phosphate and glucose-6-phosphate \[[41](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R41)\]. **More specifically, natural glucose source isolated from leaf starch of common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris) or spinach (Spinacia oleracea) is depleted in deuterium in the C(2) position. Carbon specific deuterium depletion in fatty acids from plants \[[42](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R42)\] and other sources \[[43](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R43),[44](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R44)\] is also evident, which generate deuterium depleted matrix water in mitochondria during complete oxidation in complex-IV.** Variations in carbon specific deuterium content of oxidizable substrates in the pentose and TCA cycles point to the biological role of intramolecular deuterium distributions that may ultimately be essential to understand all details of product deuterium abundances in compartmentalized deuterium transferring intracellular systems as oncogenic initiators

### Penny — 2017-12-04T21:11:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229494>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24157871/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24157871/)
**Contribution of serine, folate and glycine metabolism to the ATP, NADPH and purine requirements of cancer cells.**
[Tedeschi PM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Tedeschi%20PM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871)1, [Markert EK](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Markert%20EK%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Gounder M](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Gounder%20M%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Lin H](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lin%20H%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Dvorzhinski D](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Dvorzhinski%20D%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Dolfi SC](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Dolfi%20SC%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Chan LL](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chan%20LL%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Qiu J](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Qiu%20J%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [DiPaola RS](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=DiPaola%20RS%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Hirshfield KM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Hirshfield%20KM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Boros LG](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Boros%20LG%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Bertino JR](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Bertino%20JR%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Oltvai ZN](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Oltvai%20ZN%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Vazquez A](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Vazquez%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871).
**[Author information](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24157871/#)**
**Abstract**
Recent observations on cancer cell metabolism indicate increased serine synthesis from glucose as a marker of poor prognosis. We have predicted that a fraction of the synthesized serine is routed to a pathway for ATP production. The pathway is composed by reactions from serine synthesis, one-carbon (folate) metabolism and the glycine cleavage system (SOG pathway). Here we show that the SOG pathway is upregulated at the level of gene expression in a subset of human tumors and that its level of expression correlates with gene signatures of cell proliferation and Myc target activation. We have also estimated the SOG pathway metabolic flux in the NCI60 tumor-derived cell lines, using previously reported exchange fluxes and a personalized model of cell metabolism. We find that the estimated rates of reactions in the SOG pathway are highly correlated with the proliferation rates of these cell lines. We also observe that the SOG pathway contributes significantly to the energy requirements of biosynthesis, to the NADPH requirement for fatty acid synthesis and to the synthesis of purines. Finally, when the PC-3 prostate cancer cell line is treated with the antifolate methotrexate, we observe a decrease in the ATP levels, AMP kinase activation and a decrease in ribonucleotides and fatty acids synthesized from \[1,2-(13)C2\]-D-glucose as the single tracer. Taken together our results indicate that the SOG pathway activity increases with the rate of cell proliferation and it contributes to the biosynthetic requirements of purines, ATP and NADPH of cancer cells.

# Thread: Cholesteotoma question?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cholesteotoma-question.20533/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-02T00:46:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cholesteotoma-question.20533/post-229300>

Q: [Dr. Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/?fref=mentions) I've been reading about ear cholesteatomas and endoplasm reticulum and mithocondrial stress, do you have any comment on that?? Thanks.

ANS: Yes, It is very complicated. nnEMF and blue light increase the deuterium fractionation of tissues. Well if you are arounf a lot of nnEMF your ear pays deep attention to this because of how the reflex arc of acoustics works in the brain. You can read about this here: [https://www.jackkruse.com/cpc-14-tinnitus-quantum-view-point/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cpc-14-tinnitus-quantum-view-point/)

I always ask people with this disease about occupation and habits and if they have had tinnitus or a Bell's Palsy. I have never found one patient where this was not true. nnEMF is why I ask because it stimulate growth in the middle ear skin via reflex. You have to know how the acoustic reflex works. Inicdent light forms the afferent loop. This means humans use light to hear as the link above shows. As the nnEMF destroys the reflex arc it allows the middle ear skin to begin to collect massive amounts of deuterium and this stimulates the growth of the middle ear epithelium. Cholesteatoma is a mitochondrial disease and not a genetic one that is mediated thermodynamically by volume changes due to deuterium fractionation in the epitympanum. Throughout the history of oncology research, in both the conventional and alternative cancer research realms, there has been a cause and effect relationship that has been largely ignored. The ability of a cell to divide, whether it be a malignant (CA) or non-malignant cell (cholestetoma), is highly dependent on cell volume (deuterium fraction in tissue growing), as well as membrane potential (DHA).

The collagen tensegrity system is piezo and flexoelectric and releases and absorbs light from the sun diurnally, and this is why cell volume and cancer are related; so when you lose energy and charge in a cell, the cell, mitochondria and nuclear membrane enlarges and the signal heads to the ER via the other mitochondrial membrane to stimulate translation. The temporal sequence of the enlargement inside a cell is the critical difference. Mitochondrial morphology is one of the earliest changes because of changes in how electrons and protons are used in the matrix. Cells that are cycling (dividing), progress through the following phases: G1 (Gap 1) – this is the phase where the cell is preparing for the next phase, which is the S phase, or DNA Synthesis phase. Once DNA synthesis is complete, the cell enters the G2 phase (G 2), where it prepares to enter the final phase, called the M phase, or mitosis.

During mitosis, the cell divides into 2 cells. The cell volume is at its smallest at G1, and gradually increases its volume until it reaches its largest volume in the M phase. This should be intuitive, because the cell must become large enough to divide and then support two cells. This process is stimulated by TCA intermediates loaded with deuterium in this ear disease because nnEMF stimulate deuterium absorbtion in the middle ear. Cholesteatomas are more aggressive and have associated bony and ossicular erosive changes and arise from specific locations in the middle ear because of the volume changes in skin cells. The typical pars flaccida type cholesteatoma arises in the epitympanum. When MRI is used (rare cause CT is better) we can see sigal change in the fat signals when we vary MRI parameters. This is strong evidence for the presence of high deuterium in this area because of its kinetic isotope effect on imaging. It affects image intentsity compared to H+ fat. Deuterium has double the atomic weight the spin effect is the real problem in the middle ear. The numbers on its magnetic moment destroy chemical flux in any system via changes of the TCA cycle in the middle ear from the nnEMF effect of collecting deuterium in this region. This collection of deuterium leads to tissue swelling which stimulate the cell cycle to get to the mitosis phase and skin cells are expert at making ketatin and cholesterol. So the chronic swelling of the cell volumes there turn out the RER and make tons of the stress lipid cholesterol and it is loaded with deuterium. This is a progrowth action because of how volume is controlled. The interesting thing is that young living systems use deuterium (insulin/GH) to retain deuterium to stilmulate growth as we develop. Plants do the same to their fruits because they want birds to eat the fruit to spread the seeds. the fruit is designed to stimulate the birds growth and the growth of the seed to germinate. This is why understanding the nuanace of it is critical fro those interested in biology. Right now bio-chemists are impotent to get that nuance and why everyone has no clue about the etiology of this disease. It mimics cancer and I just did a webinar December 2017 on the mechanism. You might want to watch it at some point.

### Josh Rosenthal — 2017-12-05T23:13:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cholesteotoma-question.20533/post-229585>

is the MRI collimation that you use to see deuterium pre picture or after formatting? IE... can an MRI be reformatted afterwards to see this effect?

### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-06T00:45:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cholesteotoma-question.20533/post-229594>

Each D control's the T1 relaxation of 96 H+ atoms so the density and intensity changes is what I look for. I also look for the changes in muscle, bone and liver if I have a L spine scan.

# Thread: Hola from Oregon... for now :)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hola-from-oregon-for-now.20534/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Venessa — 2017-12-02T06:22:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hola-from-oregon-for-now.20534/post-229329>

Hello, My name's Venessa. Geez where do I start? I'll try to keep it concise...

I remember briefly coming across Dr. Kruse's site like maybe 6-7 years ago (?) but was obviously not ready for it or understood anything, as I am just now introducing myself today. Lately, I've been diving into Kruse blogs, podcasts, webinar recordings, and will take time but everything is now coming together for me - it's finally the right time (as she writes with 7 browser tabs open relating to mitochondrial health).

Background: I'm a functional nutritionist and what I've been calling "intuitive nourishment coach"... but interestingly I'm less interested in the "food" that you put in your mouth and more interested in connecting to nature and other "food" we feed our body, mind, soul the deeper I get into this work.
My family is from Puerto Rico, I was born and raised in Chicago - just moved to southern Oregon in Jan of 2017.

I got into the "alternative" health world after my mom was diagnosed with breast cancer in 2004. Knowing what I now know... she was clearly also dealing with insulin resistance, adrenal issues, hormonal imbalance, SIBO, etc etc prior to her diagnosis. Went the conventional route, did nothing for her - gave her a few months, we then did alternative route \*in sunny Arizona\* and she actually GOT BETTER. But then we had to come back to Chi and it was downhill from there. My mom passed in 2010 - a year longer than they gave her. Throughout her journey I was reading things like Becker's Body Electric and pissed that doctor's didn't seem to know anything.

I overcame insomnia, chronic acid reflux, asthma, thyroid issues with lifestyle/diet changes during her journey which prompted me to go back to school and start helping people so they didn't have to suffer same fate. I find that I learn more by experimenting with myself (like most of us here it seems! N=1) and working with people than any school program can teach.

Genetics: Haplotype L1b1a7a.
Mom always wanted to move somewhere sunny and warm, and eat Mangos and fish lol Gratitude that I know live in the countryside very near vast nature, woods, etc (coming from innercity Chicago) and my partner and I have plans to move to Mexico some day (my partner's family is from there).

Would anyone be able to point out where I could get more info on haplotypes (i.e. optimal environment and inputs for various haplotypes)? Appreciate any help on that front!

Last thing... As I started going deeper into nourishment work, and "re-wilding" myself as in connecting with nature in a deeper way, I started to get messages in dreams and Akashic Records (I'm an Akashic Record reader and intuitive in this way - btw not interested in discussions around validity of that or the woo-factor, etc). Interestingly, I started to get messages about mitochondria a while back and that I needed to look more into this. That there was an entire story that is not being "told" as it relates to the energetics that is being passed down in this way as it relates to disease. There's also something about viruses and how they play a role - I see that Dr. Kruse writes about this as well, so going to look into that more. The info that Dr. Kruse and Dr. Wallace share about mitochondria has been the most profound and in line with so many ancient "ways of knowing" that I have found so far.

The latest dream I had was of a plant that absorbed and utilized external nutrients in environment more efficiently and effectively when done in sunlight, and that we (humans) are the same way - that eating out in the sun, alfresco, has many benefits for absorption, assimilation, utilization, and function. I'm curious if this is true... if anyone knows of actual science or data in literature (or a possible explanation of mechanism) that might support this? While I'm driven by intuition, a girl's gotta have some sound evidence to backup!

Thanks for reading if you've stuck around and looking forward to connecting more with everyone

And thank you Dr. Kruse for your work and community.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-229446>

### NeilBB — 2017-12-04T02:55:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-229447>

Deuterium labelling is an old technique. But there are many obvious issues in this study. The article is about saturated fats excessively incorporated into membranes being bad, likely that is true. Where it fails is in "assuming" that eating saturated fat is what precipitates this occurrence. It is well known that this is not true. In fact, from a strict dietary standpoint, without even getting into any biophysics, it is easily demonstrated that the best way to increase circulating triglycerides (for potential incorporation into membranes) is to eat a high carbohydrate diet. The liver is expert at converting excess sugars into palmitic acid, a 16 carbon saturated fat. A high fat diet typically does the opposite, facilitating fatty acid oxidation and lowering circulating saturated fats.

### NeilBB — 2018-03-05T20:55:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-236354>

The more I have thought about this, the more firmly I believe the answer is yes and that it is highly significant and may even be confusing many of the so-called experts in this new field as well, who also BTW, have careers to build and products to sell. Caveat emptor.

I will also say this. Biology has been dealing with deuterium for a long time. Forever, in fact. Higher environmental levels than today even. So I might take a step back and consider other strategies before I would build a distillery to drink DDW 24/7 or sell my house to Qlaravia. There's a place for those tools for some for sure, but I think it's fundamentally more about biological deuterium trafficking than fanatical deuterium abstinence. Going crazy about the ppm in coffee beans seems like a gigantic waste of time to me. And I don't know that breath analysis of expired water vapor tells you anything about tissue (or more importantly, subcellular) isotopic distribution either. In fact, I seriously doubt it.

And the important distinction between matrix water and other body pools and how they interface seems to have been forgotten here lately as well. Deuterium clearly can be biologically beneficial in the right biological place/time/context as well.

Some studies have also even showed that judicious use of heavy water could prolong life in some animals in some cases. Don't you all wonder why that might be the case? I certainly have been thinking about how that might work for a while, in between jamming and posting rock videos. But that's just me. I'm odd.

And if it does, artificially deuterating a molecule with thousands of time higher concentrations of the heavier isotope should be expected to alter the result of all biological labelling studies, calling all of these results into question.

Classic example of what the quantum physicists would call the observer effect or measurement effect...but I would still argue with them as to this being primarily an epistemological rather than metaphysical issue. What is needed is a way to measure very small phenomena in a way that does not alter the system, which may in fact be impossible. But that would not mean that reality is dependent on consciousness, rather it would mean that our consciousness is simply incapable of measuring certain aspects of reality on certain scales. A important distinction IMO, one that may ultimately define the boundary limits of human knowledge.

### NeilBB — 2018-03-06T02:09:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-236382>

All of the biological deuterium labelling studies rely on the implicit assumption that scientists have devised a way to distinguish this isotope while biological processess have yet to notice it. Bad assumption.

So once you acknowledge the fact of biological impacts of deuterium, you must acknowledge it is not a passive observer (label) but an active participant.

The question of degree and context still remains however in determining how much error would be introduced into a specific experiment. The only way to know that would be to have much more detailed knowledge of the specific mechanics of deuterium sorting and flowing through all biological systems. Good luck! There are also studies that measure the isotopic hydrogen fractions in various biological systems in natural circumstances, without any artificial labelling. Those are interesting.

**Experimental and clinical evidences of deuterium depletion and cell growth control**
Consistent with the above, the effect of low 2H in water has been shown to control cell proliferation in numerous biological systems _in vitro_ and _in vivo_[\[12,25–27\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306987715004399#b0060). _In vitro_studies, in which the only difference of the growth media was 2H concentration in water, confirmed that 2H depletion inhibits cell growth in a dose dependent manner. To the contrary, increasing the 2H concentration over the natural abundance in water stimulates cell growth. The effects observed can be modeled when the growth rate of tumor cells is significantly inhibited in culture prepared with deuterium-depleted water (DDW) and, importantly, that physically restoring 2H levels by adding heavy water restores cell growth rates [\[28,29\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306987715004399#b0140)." ### NeilBB — 2018-03-07T18:46:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-236591>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=236589)
>
> Ethanol C2H6O
>
> I bet some of those H in ethanol are actually D's.
>
> So, the question is:
> how to make DDE, Deuterium Depleted Ethanol.
>
> ........

I've been looking into this for some time, actually, lol. It's not that easy to determine. Conflicting info.

On the one hand, ethanol is almost always naturally fermented by yeast, which involves glycolysis, which in theory should deplete some of the deuterium from the sugar substrates. But on the other hand, it's only yeast glycolysis/fermentation...

Some chemistry research uses deuterium ratios to try to detect adulterated alcohol (and food products) and assume that the products of fermentation should have similar D/H ratios as the substrate carbohydrate it was fermented from. It may or may not be a correct assumption.

Other relevant facts:

Wine grapes, barley (Scotch malt), and rye are products of C3 photosynthesis.

Corn (bourbon) and sugarcane (rum, cachaca) are products of C4 photosynthesis.

Agave (Tequila, Mezcal) is a product of CAM photosynthesis.

Distillation of ethanol/water mix left at cask strength would have to be relatively depleted compared to other preparations.

The water in Scotland is said to be fairly low in deuterium due to the climate.

Do with that what you will and let me know when your distillery becomes operational.

# Thread: what was something you did that surprised you at how well it improved your sleeP/
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/>

**Posts:** 27

### taiyang — 2017-12-03T14:16:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229408>

my sleep is still hit or miss because i might get hot or have insomnia. there are a lot of generally accepted ways to improve sleep. what is one thing you did that really surprised you at how much it improved your sleep? also could you be specific as to how much you did of this and how long it took to see results? please don't mention things like blacking out the bedroom, regular bed times, not eating large meals at night, not having a cell phone in your room, as these are things i already do. i also restrict blue light at night, but it seems not to be enough.
thanks.

### shiran — 2017-12-03T14:48:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229410>

I didn't sleep for 7 years because I kept my son alive... I literally kept him alive at night ( read my journal don't ask me why)
I can tell you one thing for sure, sleep is a huge huge deal.
What I learned this 2017 year thanks to Dr jack Kruse is :
Sleep starts at sunrise.

### taiyang — 2017-12-03T16:24:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229414>

you probably had to fight to stay awake, i have to fight to sleep- different. i know sleep is a big deal and i know melatonin is produced in the eye starting a sunrise. i get 30 min of am sun now. that is not enough. i need to increase the amount or most likely do other things in conjunction with the 30 min. i am trying to cut down on screen time so i can only read things of direct relevance to me and my key sleep issue, which seems to be different from your sleep issue. thanks anyway. ps i hope members are not picking up on the habit of being enigmatic in their replies. if you are going to be so, please don't reply to my questions. my screen time is limited. at the same time, i will try to do my due diligence and read up on my issues as much as possible.

### Lahelada — 2017-12-03T17:50:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229418>

Tai, my time is limited, as I want to sleep too.  How do you know what is relevant ? Anything you know already is up for scrutiny.
You say "no large meals at night already doing that". I will mention "**eat in the red** !" So now you could say I eat my latest meal at ... pm and then this would be a very relevant conversation. Maybe you are living under the misconception that eating 4 hours before bed is enough ? CW says so. It is, if bed time is early enough but not if you go to bed at 1pm .

**Are you grounding in your 30 minutes in the sun ? When do your 30 minutes start? Is Uv already present in your location? Maybe you start too late.** You see the forbidden needs to be mentioned for clarity.

You could look into **strength exercises before bed**. **CT ?** Are you doing it? Please give details in your questions so that we can judge if what you do not want mentioned needs to be mentioned. Better still,start a diary showing what you actually do in detail. For example do you get late **pm sun** ? Do you know why you should get it yet ? **"Heal in the red !"**

Radical suggestion 1 : Do more of what you are already doing .
Radical suggestion 2 : Go away to do it somewhere else exactly the way you do it now. It may tell you whether you are in the right place where you live.

### Mystic Rose60 — 2017-12-03T22:49:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229435>

What worked for me was simple. I threw away all of my sunglasses and made it a priority to see every morning sunrise and get at least 30 to 60minutes or more of full body(skin surfaces) nude sun exposure, no excuses.This is how you make the sleep hormone, melatonoin and also, dopamine. I also make a point to watch evening sunsets and make more. I also threw away my melatonin supplement that I was addicted to for many years. Within in matter of several weeks, I became a very solid, sound sleeper and to this day, I go to bed when the sun goes down and I sleep for 7 1/2 to 9 hours, averaging right around 8 though. We also have a kill switch to the electricity in our bedroom and that is very crucial for a quality night's sleep.

### taiyang — 2017-12-04T02:07:52+0200

mystic, where do you live? if i were to go to bed when the sun goes down, i wuld be sleeping around 6 pm. thanks for the ideas everyone. i have an idea to sleep in a tent in my backyard at least two days a week when the weather is warmer. i wish there were more uv transparent clothes to buy for warmer weather- not just swim suits.

### Sue-UK — 2017-12-04T11:00:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229460>

I'm in the UK and its dark by 5 p.m., winter low light levels generally, and cold. No computer after sunset. I'm in bed by 8 most nights, (5 nights a week minimum) black out curtains. For the first few nights I lay awake in the dark for what seemed like ages (I don't use a clock - clock watching makes my sleep worse), but for trying to get sleepy and if I woke up in the night, I would just try and keep in a calm, meditative state. Within a few days my brain seemed to get used to the new rhythm, and I fall asleep quite quickly now, and very rarely wake up in the night. With sunset so early, I don't eat after 4 pm, some days its around 2 pm. I'm rural, extremely low meter readings, don't have wifi on etc, no street lights. Grounding is critical for me, so I'm using a grounding yoga mat as a runner on the bottom of the bed, so my feet and calves are on it, going out to a grounding rod outside the bedroom window. Before I got the mat, if I woke in the night and couldn't get back to sleep, I would go outside in the dark and ground for a while and then go back to bed. That helped, so I realised how important grounding is to my n=1. When I go to bed I'm using a migraine cap from the freezer to cool the CSF on my head. I take it off either when it loses its coldness if I'm not asleep, or when I start feeling really drowsy. I'm still using summer bedding, difficult at first when the weather suddenly got colder, but I stuck with it and now it seems normal. I'm out for the sunrise, grounding. For the cold I've got a kaftan made out of 100% cotton cellular cot blankets, which gives me some warmth but the holes let in more light. When I can I do my outside CT to later in the morning when its a bit brighter. The water is very cold, but if there's some good winter sunshine, I do a session floating on the surface, then dunking down, and then dry off nude in the sun. The sun feels hotter because my skin surfaces are so cold, and it feels awesome! When the weather is crap and because of what I'm trying to achieve for my own n=1 I do use therapeutic lights some days, and do some specific hacks, (but as treatments not lifestyle choices), but they are add ons. My best sleep in a low quantum yield is grounded, cold, dark and boring.

### taiyang — 2017-12-04T12:09:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229468>

Thanks, Sue. How could I learn to ground with a yoga mat? are there videos on youtube? i read earlier someone's explanation of how they did something similar, and it did not give me a clear picture. i also go to bed around 8 pm and get up at around 4 am. to deal with all the lights in my house (because no one in the house believes in what i am doing), i have to completely cover myself, including gloves, very wide-brimmed hat, long sleeves, turtle neck, pants, socks, uvex glasses. how do you deal with the time from 5pm to 8pm when it is dark, but you haven't gone to sleep yet? do you have special lighting for that time? if so, could you recommend a brand? when doing grounding in cold weather, is it better to be walking barefoot or just sitting, or is really doesn't matter? we have 24/7 wifi with no one but me willing to change. i think this environment is also affecting my 20 year old daughter, so i need to do things like the yoga mat or a magnetico for her or special lights that would allow her to do homework, but be less harmful than regular lighting, because she would probably accept that if she saw some improvement in her sleep and overall health. she has a bucket handle tear of her meniscus and needs surgery on december 19. she had surgery on the same knee 2 years ago. i am concerned her health problems will just get worse and worse. i need all the hacks i can get for this type of person- i cannot force them, but if i do things that don't require them to change their lifestyle that much, it's a beginning. i hope doing hacks for her will let her see improvements, and then cause her to accept more difficult change, like becoming an early bird instead of a night owl.

### Sue-UK — 2017-12-04T13:36:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229469>

I didn't need to learn to ground with the mat, I just make sure its connected, and get into bed. I wouldn't use it connected to house wiring though, for me it has to go out to the earth. (If I lived in a higher non native emf environment I'd probably go for a magnetico). Grounding outside could be standing, sitting or walking, or stretched out on the grass in good weather (belly grounding is great ....), I just have to do it ....

The biggest thing for me for helping family members "see" what I was trying to achieve was getting a meter. There's some that are better than others for measuring different things, I just have a Cornet 88T, but its one of my better purchases this year. It helps to know what I'm dealing with, and DH can't argue the numbers. My youngest left home in September, but when he was living here I did compromise, but only up to a point. Me and DH pay the bills, so we make the rules when it comes to wifi. When the kids are home the wifi is on if they are indoors, but they have to switch it off at night. Two strikes and they are out - if I find its been left on when I get up at sunrise, they lose a strike. Twice and it goes off when I go to bed, and they know I mean it.  I can do all the hacks I want, but if I'm being zapped by wifi 24/7, and particularly at night, I could be limiting the effectiveness of everything else I do, or need to do a helluva lot more to mitigate it, and I'm not gonna do more to stand still .....If I do more its to move forward ..... Lights after sunset are dim and towards the red, and if the tv is on I'm at least covered up and blue blocking. (An n=1 hack on my own blockers for that). Its not ideal, and I've yet to find an ambient light source that I'm happy with using chronically in the evening (even red light) which is why the cold, dark and boring from 8 p.m. is critical. Just enough light to be able to move around the house without walking into things. I don't do any housework or washing up after sunset, if I can see to clean something properly, its too bright ... It can wait. It may sound selfish but I've concentrated on me, not wasted energy trying to convince my adult children .... As my health and mental functioning improved, they started asking questions about what I'm doing ...

### Mystic Rose60 — 2017-12-04T14:00:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229470>

> [taiyang said:](/goto/post?id=229445)
>
> mystic, where do you live? if i were to go to bed when the sun goes down, i wuld be sleeping around 6 pm. thanks for the ideas everyone. i have an idea to sleep in a tent in my backyard at least two days a week when the weather is warmer. i wish there were more uv transparent clothes to buy for warmer weather- not just swim suits.

I'm in Michigan @[taiyang](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20771/) . During the long cold and dark winter months, I am in bed oince it gets dark. Once I awaken though, I will just lay there until my wind up clock says 4 a.m. I use red light, candles and the fireplace in the winter and if I get on my computer, my skin surfaces are all covered and I have red and black light behind me and IRIS software that is set to sleep, always.

Sleeping in a tent is a great idea, but make sure you are not sleeping over any nnemf that may be underground or overhead. Do you have the right meter to test this? Also, make sure you are not exposed to blue light (nnemf). I slept in a tent a lot during my first year and it was great! I cut out the floor of my tent and slept on and in a wool sleeping bag during the spring, summer, fall and winter months, away from my home and in an area of our property that we keep wild and the critters come out at night and like it there too. I already was sleeeping in the cold during the winter for many years, as we always keep our bedroom windows open.

It's important to keep experimenting to see what works for you taiyang, Nature being the most crucial and ultimate healer there is though. If you do the work, Nature will not let you down and I say that with great confidence my dear.

### taiyang — 2017-12-06T12:35:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229623>

is there any consensus here on a good honda civic-quality meter? i'm not looking for mercedes level or a ford-pinto one either.

### Inger — 2017-12-06T13:25:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229627>

Cutting the electric breaker for the bedroom

Eliminating the wifi from the neighbors to a great degree through plastering my walls on the side the router was on with 4 fold alufoil

Always getting the morning light/sun on my eyes/body. I sleep right by the open window no curtains, so I wake up and watch into the sky in the direction where sun rises. Every morning. In winter it is so cozy in a ice cold room under a warm blanket.. to still connect with the sky and sun and trees outside.. In summer I go outside as soon as I wake up. I hate to sleep with closed window where I cant see the sky. I need that connection so badly. It is like coming home.

Eating my latest meal as early as possible, the best sleep I get when eating not after 3 PM, usually I eat my latest meal at 4 PM though but I need to do 3 PM more often!

Not drinking alcohol, especially not at night, also no coffee after 2 PM

Being outside nude and grounded in the sun from sunrise forward as many hours as possible

Cutting breaker early at 7 PM and being in the dark and silence with 1 candle reading a book like Osho or other deep, meditative stuff that makes you feel grounded and peaceful and happy. Doing this religiously a few hours before bed

That was a few things that comes to my mind that all changed my sleep to the better

### taiyang — 2017-12-06T13:47:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229631>

i need to find the breaker for my bedroom- great idea. i'm also interested in the alum foil. how did you get the foil to stick?? is the shiny side facing out or in? any other links where i could get more info on on the alum foil?

### Inger — 2017-12-06T13:58:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229637>

I just used this tape one uses when painting the walls, you know? I just taped a little here and there, it sticks well as it is not heavy. I think I did the shiny side out but I am not sure anymore..lol. In the end I covered the whole wall with wallpaper, a light type of it. I just used wallpaper paste to fix it on the alufoil. So now the wall looks pretty normal  Ok a little weird it looks but I do not care.

But I did feel weird a few days after doing that work. My hands got kinda swollen and I felt a little off. I guess I should have used gloves? I wonder if the aluminium can stick to your skin and travel inside your body? At least if doing it again I would protect me better... I just used bare hands you know.. and it was a lot of work as I did the wall 4 times over!
Do not do this if you are not sure where the radiation comes from tho. That could make it even worse! And test with a meter to check that you are not produing a microvawe oven in the process
I used the wifi signals on my laptop to check how much weaker the signal got and it was a significant decrease. But I have only 2 walls plastered, opposite walls, that is where the radiation comes from. Otherwise here is quite a little nnEMF so I felt safe doing it. I would not create a faraday cage, that is too risky I guess. If it is not 100% right done it might make the nnEMF trapped inside and just make it worse!

### philip malone — 2017-12-06T18:21:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229653>

A lot of good info here - I'll just add that on top of low emf and no blu light in the evening, I found an eye mask to help me a lot. You want absolutely no light when you sleep....

### Mystic Rose60 — 2017-12-06T18:58:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-was-something-you-did-that-surprised-you-at-how-well-it-improved-your-sleep.20538/post-229660>

> [philip malone said:](/goto/post?id=229653)
>
> A lot of good info here - I'll just add that on top of low emf and no blu light in the evening, I found an eye mask to help me a lot. You want absolutely no light when you sleep....

Blackout curtains are a good investment too. You don't want light on any of your skin surfaces while you are sleeping. My room is so dark, I can't see my fingers in front of my nose at night. Target and many other retailers carry them now.

I also have the head of our bed elevated by 6" I found bed risers at Target as well

First night with some 5 inch riser bed elevation. The ones I ordered are stackable, could do a 5, 3 or an 8, but trying the 5 inch first. Finished eating by 4, I went to sleep in a blacked out room, (open window, coldest night so far), summer covers and after using a migraine cap from the freezer to cool my CSF for 20 mins. Pilot light kicking in, reckon I slept from about 8.30 pm to around 5 am. Then drank some water and went back to sleep for a couple more hours (which I wouldn't normally do if I go to bed so early). For all the eye rolling and moaning about sleeping in such a cold room, and sliding down the bed, DH didn't need to get up in the night to pee last night. Interesting .....

# Thread: Hello From East Tennessee
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Angie Medler — 2017-12-03T19:58:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/post-229429>

I started reading Dr. Jack Kruse a few months ago and was totally overwhelmed but know that he speaks the truth. Little by little I am putting the pieces together.

I wish I had found Dr. Jack Kruse! earlier! We moved to E. Tennessee from Washington St. in 2015. I had many must haves for the place we moved and sunrise and sunset was one of the must haves I didn't get  We live in a valley but it runs in a way the sun hits our place for a very good part of the day. I don't have big power lines, I don't have traffic, I don't have cell towers, I don't have a lot of neighbors and it is a beautiful location with ponds and trees etc... We remodeled the house and oh how I wish I had known not to put in LED lights. And if I had known better, we would have looked a little further south for our place. But here we are and I need to learn how to make the most of it for now.

My husband had a heart valve replacement in June of last year. It was defective, very bad, the surgeon said it was about the worst he had seen. This was congenital and yet the drs and dietitians wanted him doing no salt low fat yada yada even though they all admitted his arteries looked good and his numbers were excellent. (Dean had cancer in 2000 before we met. Testicular that spread to kidney and he went through chemo. He has been clear of any cancer since.) The only thing he is on is Warfarin and it would be awesome to get him off of that.

Me. I'm tired of being fat. I'm tired of trying so hard and having what works for everyone else fail miserably. I did HCG for 7 months, lost 20 lbs in the first month and nothing else. 500 freakin calories a day and I could not lose weight. I can't eat wheat or grains or dairy and it is getting to where I can't eat anything without feeling bad.

I watch the Vermont 2017 talk. Wow. Even though I am outside every morning at dawn to let chickens out and take care of my horse, I have my glasses on. I don't get skin exposed because all my life with horses and animals and farm work, I don't wear shorts.

But now! Deuterium....wow. I deuterium bomb myself most days with things that are "good for me". I feel like this might be the biggest key I have been looking for. I know that I'm missing something.

Haplogroup is U2e2
I will attach my Methylation Profile

Dean is Haplogroup H5

That's me. For the time being I will have to be a lion or a hippo but I am learning!

Angie and Dean

### caroline — 2017-12-04T10:40:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/post-229456>

Hi Angie and welcome.......

Have you read the leptin Reset? the C/T protocol?

You need to get sun on more of your skin/body. Why not throw on shorts and a tank top and get outside for sunrise? Even better - can you get outside naked? You must get sun on your body.

Do you have a kiniki swimsuit?

Give Dr. K. your Mom's and Gran's health history.

What are you eating? drinking?

### Lahelada — 2017-12-04T11:43:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/post-229464>

Hi Angie,welcome. I might add another missing piece to the puzzle. Have a read of CT 4 . It explains about HCG ,why it works and why it fails. If you can see the sun from your house as it rises with your bare feet planted on a patch of grass you are not so badly off.Yu probably would have been better further south but the rest of your living conditions do not sound bad at all. However, exposing flesh would be great,especially the ladies. As you are up with the chickens anyway it would be a shame to miss that opportunity.

The LED lights have remedy. Use them only when you NEED to see. You may even be able to change them out for yellow ones or even red ones. Red would be better but I am not sure if one can get them. For evenings candlelight is best. Failing that you could use floorlamps and table lamps with red bulbs. Low level of light though. Do not have them at eye level though or in line of sight where you spend most of your time.

Do you use IRIS on your tech gear screens? It is a free app in its basic form iristech.co

Seconding what Caroline said. Give a history of your Gran and your Mum and your history paying special attention to your first 6 years of life.

### Angie Medler — 2017-12-05T17:01:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/post-229542>

Thank you ladies for the great info and advice!

I did read the Leptin Reset, I purchased the EpiPaleo book a few months ago. I'm overwhelmed with where to even begin.

I cannot see the sunrise from the house, or the sunset, we are in the hills (mountains). We just built a chicken coop that may have given us our naked sunny place where the neighbors can't see. Its crazy, but it is illegal to be exposed in TN  I will look in to the kiniki swimsuit for sure and work on exposing more skin.

We eat organic, grass fed, pasture raised. I know who is raising my food and how they were processed. I feed my birds (and horse) organic feed soy and wheat free so that my eggs don't have wheat. Unfortunate that we don't like seafood, so we are going to have to find a way to get good quality seafood in to us. I don't eat dairy (even though we get raw grass fed cow milk here, I do use the butter though), no soy, no wheat or grains, no nightshades and sugars are rare. Potatoes make my ankles hurt and weak immediately.

Well water through a water softener and filtration system with UV light. The water here has a lot of iron and sulfur.

Mom is in good health, thyroid issue is the only think I know of for her. I don't remember much about my first 6 years, I was the first born of 4 in WI, we moved a lot, lived in Montana, Minnesota, Illinois and Wisconsin during that time. Mom's mom died when she was very young, we knew nothing about her until recently when I was able to track down the family that she came from via my DNA and people that are related and looking for their relatives. She was born near Kingsport TN (near me now), she was married when she was 13 and had 3 kids. Her 2 boys died within a year of each other from VEO-Colitis. She ran off and married my grandfather near Chicago and then another son die in a stock tank on the farm. She had 6 more kids and died from exhaustion or bad blood transfusion.

I've put some red bulbs in several rooms, it helps when we are up 2 hours before the sun comes up.

### Inger — 2017-12-05T19:34:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/post-229560>

Welcome Angie
You will still get light even if you cant see the sun.. which helps for sure. But maybe cou could rive a bit or bike to a spot where you can see the sunrises? At least a few times / week?
Great to have a place no one can see for nude tanning! It helps huge   I do not think it is bothering anyone when you build a space where no neighbors can see you

### caroline — 2017-12-05T23:00:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-east-tennessee.20539/post-229583>

Don't be overwhelmed - Jack is all about the three legged stool .......light water and magnetism.

Follow the Patreon blogs and podcasts - especially the Vermont ones and you will get caught up really fast.

Become a gold member if you can manage it and you will have access to all the blogs and the monthly live Q&A when you can ask Jack anything.

# Thread: Stuttering
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ozbuck — 2017-12-04T01:02:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/post-229442>

Hey,

I'm wondering if Jack or anyone else has any ideas/experience on what causes stuttering and possible tactics to overcome it.

Obviously there's something going on in the neural networks of the brain so I'm interested in any insights people may have in regards to this. It's interesting to note that 99% of people who chronically stutter don't stutter when they talk to a plant, or a dog, or in a room by themselves. It seems that a perceived stress (social anxiety) interacts with the pre-setup neural network to create the stuttering.

Also, many people who stutter who are in a state of full confidence, love, etc find their stuttering greatly diminishes or, even in some cases, disappears completely when in those states.

Any ideas, thoughts, experiences?

Thanks
Oz

### taiyang — 2017-12-16T21:38:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/post-230438>

my husband has Tourette syndrome with stuttering. with age both his vocal and muscle tics have gone down. he can repeat a word like 4 times. it usually is more pronounced in a slightly stressful situation.

### htw — 2017-12-16T22:26:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/post-230439>

my stuttering was huge but now is little, I'm laughing at it now. In my case SCN (dopamine ale melatonin) was issue. Now I love to talk

### nonchalant — 2017-12-16T23:22:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/post-230440>

Ozbuck, following Jack's protocols does reduce anxiety, whether social or physical.

### taiyang — 2017-12-17T01:34:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/post-230456>

my husband was made fun of at his work. then he asked his doctor for medication to control the tics and was prescribed halidol, which stopped the tics but made him feel like a zombie. he decided it wasn't worth it. he just accepts the way he acts and doesn't care too much what others think. he will try to control himself out in public, but then when we get to the parking lot, he will start making sounds like a horse. he used to have an explosive temper and got in streets fights and was beat up himself. that's improved with age too. i like to say that the more his hair falls out, the better he gets

### taiyang — 2017-12-17T01:53:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuttering.20541/post-230460>

i have always thought my husband really macho because he had all these tics and still said what he wanted to say. he takes no crap. he is about 5 foot 6 inches. once he was in a train station talking with his friend in Chinese, their native language. this tall white american dude towers over my husband and rudely says that this is america- speak english. my husband curses at him in his chinglish and said- this is american- i have the freedom of speak! i have too many classic stories like that.

his tourette symptoms really took off at puberty and have subsided with age, so i think in his case high testosterone is involved. he also used to be a chain smoker and big drinker, but now he has replaced that with pokemon hunting, so it's all good.

# Thread: Work Environment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kris90 — 2017-12-04T17:38:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229481>

I need to pose an important question for a mitochondriac regarding work environment.

Would a mitochondriac choose a job:

1)
\-Work location is close to home (30 min commute) and free parking
\-Office is located outside of downtown core of major city (lower population density)
\-Office is a 2 story building (working on ground level)
\-Atmosphere is more "proper", the people are less fun to work with (energy vampires) and the work is not very exciting
\-Strict telecommute (work from home) policy (only as a case-by-case need)

2)
\-Work location is farther from home (45 min commute) and parking would be $100/month minimum
\-Office is located in the centre of downtown core (very high population density)
\-Office is in a high-rise building, likely between floors 5 and 16
\-Atmosphere is super chill, the people are a blast to work with (used to work with them, energy boosters) and the projects are super exciting and engaging
\-Flexible telecommute (possibility to work from home 1-2x per week, or as needed)

Salary is the same, but of course 2) requires more $ for gas and parking.

### WalterNL — 2017-12-04T18:09:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229482>

Interesting choices. You have to grade all factors as to how bad/good they really are. Maybe both offices are equally bad in terms of nnEMF, and working home cuts down the total time spent commuting for the second job compared to the first. If there is no real benefit to the first office I'd go with the second. And given that the group are energy vampires, I assume their office isn't good at all.

### kris90 — 2017-12-04T18:19:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229483>

Thanks Walter, I have a similar analysis: an office job is an office job. Poor lighting, and nnEMF regardless. Working from home is a huge plus since my home environment is literally as optimal as can be, so I think I need that flexibility.

Also, the work I do and the people I work with seems to have a major impact on my mood, energy and wellbeing. Working on boring shit all day with boring people = exhausted by the end of the day. Working on cool shit with hilarious people acting like their true selves = feeling energized by end of day, and have funny stories to share with my wife over some malbec when the kids are in bed. If it means a little more EMF, I think I can biohack that.

### shiran — 2017-12-04T18:24:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229484>

Remember dopamine it's a huge issue looking into someone's eyes is so important for wellness.
I trying to look for the ideal work place in nature with nice people... it's not that easy...
I would like to work in a place like the farm Jack is creating...

### kris90 — 2017-12-04T18:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229485>

Yes I would love to get out of office/desk job and out into nature. I do take a morning and afternoon walk and spend an hour on my lunch outside, so atleast I'm providing myself "recess" like a child gets in school. But until I figure out a new career path, I'll just have to make the best of it.

Working with the right people has made a HUGE difference for me. You are right, the dopamine yield or decline from people is important. I can boost my own dopamine easily, but I think we all need human interaction. I was happy having fun with my old work buds, and unfortunately I got blindsided with the work location and convenience that I threw that away.

I've never felt my dopamine levels soar higher than when I spent a weekend in Vermont with Jack. Holy shit, I felt like I was on a crazy high for 48 hours, and it even continued after I returned home that weekend.

### kris90 — 2017-12-04T18:41:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229486>

Another major thought to consider: at my current work location, I'm about 15 min drive from a beach so I can soak up some sun to get my Vit D up in the summer.

Unfortunately, working downtown, I would not have such benefit, because I would have pay-as-you-go parking, so it would be way to expensive to drive there on my lunch everyday. My Vit D levels would likely suffer.

### Pogo — 2017-12-05T04:00:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/work-environment.20542/post-229518>

I would do whatever I could to make that:

> 2)
> \-Atmosphere is super chill, the people are a blast to work with (used to work with them, energy boosters) and the projects are super exciting and engaging
> \-Flexible telecommute (possibility to work from home 1-2x per week, or as needed)

work for me. Given the alternative, what is the point of any of it?

Get your vit-D in the mornings, on your telecommute days (work at the beach?), and the weekend.

# Thread: LED question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/led-question.20543/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### shannoncorrin — 2017-12-04T19:20:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/led-question.20543/post-229487>

hello,
This is my first time posting a question and I'm nervous, but I felt it was an appropriate one. Being an optometrist, I find this topic of light so fascinating. I have been listening to a few of Leanne Veniers talks. She said that the newer warmer LEDs are not bad to use during the day and have much lower blue light admission. She doesn't advocate wearing blue blockers all day long. Since my exam rooms have no natural light and I'm on a computer, I've been wearing my blutechs while inside, and no glasses outside. I wear them all the time at night and try to keep things dim at home. I just was wondering if anyone had any thoughts on the new LEDs? If using those and f.lux on your computer and nightshift on your phone, does it reduce blue light enough?

### shiran — 2017-12-04T20:36:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/led-question.20543/post-229489>

Hi welcome
after sunset you can go with the :
Uvex
Another good option is :
[https://raoptics.io/](https://raoptics.io/)

During the day if you are under blue lighting, it's best to protect your eyes and skin . Raoptics has good solutions

For the computer a program called IRIS is good

If using those and f.lux on your computer and nightshift on your phone, does it reduce blue light enough?

She is no expert on blue light. She has no clue how it effects deuterium. Blue light and nnEMF from tech devices increase the amount of deuterium in your tissues and decrease the amount of H+ in your tissues and you mitochondrial matrix. Your eyes get 40% of your circulating blood volume when bright light is shined in your retinas from tech devices. So if you want to know where those cataracts and AMD are coming from just stop at the deuterium step right there. Blue light REALLY changes the flow of protons (H+) in the mitochondrial matrix by allowing too much deuterium in to replace H+ massively slowing the TCA intermediates (6 x slower) turning them from a cycle into a linear pathway where the individual substrates become locked in and bound like putting super glue into a gear shift because of how the kinetic isotope effects the bonding strength between Carbon and D, Nitrogen and D, and Oxygen and D.
